infobox.value: (**C**), or the **centre**, also known as the **five**, the **big** or the **pivot**, is one of the five [positions](/wiki/Basketball_position "Basketball position") in a regulation [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") game. The center is almost always the tallest player on the team, and often has a great deal of strength and body mass as well. In the [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA"), the center is typically close to 7 feet (2.13 m) tall; centers in the [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA") are typically above 6 feet 4 inches (1.93 m). Centers traditionally play close to the basket in the [low post](/wiki/Low_post "Low post"). The two tallest players in NBA history, [Manute Bol](/wiki/Manute_Bol "Manute Bol") and [Gheorghe Mureșan](/wiki/Gheorghe_Mure%C8%99an "Gheorghe Mureșan"), were both centers, each standing 7 feet 7 inches (2.31 m) tall.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Position in basketball

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Kareem-Abdul-Jabbar_Lipofsky_%28HQ%29.jpg/250px-Kareem-Abdul-Jabbar_Lipofsky_%28HQ%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kareem-Abdul-Jabbar_Lipofsky_(HQ).jpg)

Center [Kareem Abdul-Jabbar](/wiki/Kareem_Abdul-Jabbar "Kareem Abdul-Jabbar") performs his signature shot, the [skyhook](/wiki/Hook_shot "Hook shot"), against the Lakers' rivals, the Boston Celtics.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/A%27ja_Wilson.jpg/250px-A%27ja_Wilson.jpg)](/wiki/File:A%27ja_Wilson.jpg)

Center [A'ja Wilson](/wiki/A%27ja_Wilson "A'ja Wilson") led the [Las Vegas Aces](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Aces "Las Vegas Aces") to back-to-back [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA") titles in 2022 and 2023, and again in 2025.

The **center** (**C**), or the **centre**, also known as the **five**, the **big** or the **pivot**, is one of the five [positions](/wiki/Basketball_position "Basketball position") in a regulation [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") game. The center is almost always the tallest player on the team, and often has a great deal of strength and body mass as well. In the [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA"), the center is typically close to 7 feet (2.13 m) tall; centers in the [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA") are typically above 6 feet 4 inches (1.93 m). Centers traditionally play close to the basket in the [low post](/wiki/Low_post "Low post"). The two tallest players in NBA history, [Manute Bol](/wiki/Manute_Bol "Manute Bol") and [Gheorghe Mureșan](/wiki/Gheorghe_Mure%C8%99an "Gheorghe Mureșan"), were both centers, each standing 7 feet 7 inches (2.31 m) tall.

Centers are valued for their ability to protect their own goal from high-percentage close attempts on defense, while scoring and rebounding with high efficiency on offense. In the 1950s and 1960s, [George Mikan](/wiki/George_Mikan "George Mikan") and [Bill Russell](/wiki/Bill_Russell "Bill Russell") were centerpieces of championship dynasties and defined early prototypical centers. With the addition of a [three-point field goal](/wiki/Three-point_field_goal "Three-point field goal") for the [1979–80 season](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_NBA_season "1979–80 NBA season"), however, NBA basketball gradually became more perimeter-oriented and saw the importance of a traditional center diminish.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Modern day big men include elite playmakers and passers such as [Nikola Jokić](/wiki/Nikola_Joki%C4%87 "Nikola Jokić") and versatile scorers such as [Bam Adebayo](/wiki/Bam_Adebayo "Bam Adebayo") and [Victor Wembanyama](/wiki/Victor_Wembanyama "Victor Wembanyama").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Center_(basketball)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1940s–1950s: Emergence and George Mikan era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Center_(basketball)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1940s–1950s: Emergence and George Mikan era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/GeorgeMikan.jpg/250px-GeorgeMikan.jpg)](/wiki/File:GeorgeMikan.jpg)

[George Mikan](/wiki/George_Mikan "George Mikan") with No. 99

The center is considered a necessary component for a successful team, especially in professional leagues such as the NBA. Great centers have been the foundation for most of the dynasties in both the NBA and NCAA. Until the 1940s, even dominant centers such as [Moose Krause](/wiki/Moose_Krause "Moose Krause") were not extraordinary tall. Then, the 6 ft 10 in (2.08 m) [George Mikan](/wiki/George_Mikan "George Mikan") and the 7 ft 0 in (2.13 m) [Bob Kurland](/wiki/Bob_Kurland "Bob Kurland") pioneered as exceptionally tall centers, shattering the widely held perception that tall players could not develop the agility and coordination to play basketball well, and ushering in the role of the dominant big man. While Kurland never played professional basketball after his time at [Oklahoma State](/wiki/Oklahoma_State_Cowboys_men%27s_basketball "Oklahoma State Cowboys men's basketball"), Mikan turned professional in 1946 after leading [DePaul](/wiki/DePaul_Blue_Demons_men%27s_basketball "DePaul Blue Demons men's basketball") to the [NIT](/wiki/National_Invitation_Tournament "National Invitation Tournament") title. He went on to win seven [National Basketball League](/wiki/National_Basketball_League_(United_States) "National Basketball League (United States)"), [Basketball Association of America](/wiki/Basketball_Association_of_America "Basketball Association of America") and [NBA Championships](/wiki/NBA_Championship "NBA Championship") in his ten-year career (1946–56), nine of them with the [Minneapolis Lakers](/wiki/Minneapolis_Lakers "Minneapolis Lakers"). Using his height to dominate opposing players, Mikan invented the [hook shot](/wiki/Hook_shot "Hook shot") and the [shot block](/wiki/Block_(basketball) "Block (basketball)"); as a consequence, the NCAA, and later NBA, adopted the [goaltending](/wiki/Goaltending "Goaltending") rule, and in 1951 the NBA widened the foul lane, a decision known as the Mikan rule.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### 1960s: Bill Russell–Wilt Chamberlain era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Center_(basketball)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1960s: Bill Russell–Wilt Chamberlain era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Wilt_Chamberlain_Bill_Russell_%28lighting_fixed%29.jpg/250px-Wilt_Chamberlain_Bill_Russell_%28lighting_fixed%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilt_Chamberlain_Bill_Russell_(lighting_fixed).jpg)

[Bill Russell](/wiki/Bill_Russell "Bill Russell") (left) and [Wilt Chamberlain](/wiki/Wilt_Chamberlain "Wilt Chamberlain") (right)

In the 1960s, [Bill Russell](/wiki/Bill_Russell "Bill Russell") and [Wilt Chamberlain](/wiki/Wilt_Chamberlain "Wilt Chamberlain") further transformed basketball by combining height with a greater level of athleticism than previous centers. Following the retirement of [George Mikan](/wiki/George_Mikan "George Mikan"), the rivalry of the two big men came to dominate the NBA. Between the two of them, Chamberlain and Russell won nine of the eleven [MVP](/wiki/NBA_Most_Valuable_Player "NBA Most Valuable Player") awards in the eleven-year period between 1958 and 1969; played against one another in six Eastern Conference Finals and two NBA Finals between 1959 and 1969. Many of the records set by these two players have endured today. Most notably, Chamberlain and Russell hold [the top eighteen season averages for rebounds](/wiki/List_of_National_Basketball_Association_top_individual_rebounding_season_averages "List of National Basketball Association top individual rebounding season averages").

[Bill Russell](/wiki/Bill_Russell "Bill Russell") led the [University of San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco_Dons "San Francisco Dons") to two consecutive [NCAA championships](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Men%27s_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Division I Men's Basketball Championship") (1955, 56). He joined the [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics") and helped make them one of the greatest dynasties in NBA history, winning eleven championships over his thirteen-year career (1956–69) as well as five MVP awards. Russell revolutionized defensive strategy with his shot-blocking, rebounding and physical [man-to-man defense](/wiki/Man-to-man_defense "Man-to-man defense"). While he was never the focal point of the Celtics offense, much of the team's scoring came when Russell grabbed defensive rebounds and initiated [fast breaks](/wiki/Fast_break "Fast break") with precision outlet passes, primarily to [point guard](/wiki/Point_guard "Point guard") [Bob Cousy](/wiki/Bob_Cousy "Bob Cousy"). As the NBA's first African-American superstar, Russell struggled throughout his career with the racism he encountered from fans in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), particularly after the 1966–67 season, when he became the first African-American in any major sport to be named [player-coach](/wiki/Player-coach "Player-coach").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

His principal rival [Wilt Chamberlain](/wiki/Wilt_Chamberlain "Wilt Chamberlain"), listed at 7 ft 1 in (2.16 m), 275 pounds (125 kg), lacked Russell's supporting cast. Chamberlain played college ball for the [Kansas Jayhawks](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks_men%27s_basketball "Kansas Jayhawks men's basketball"), leading them to the 1957 title game against the [North Carolina Tar Heels](/wiki/North_Carolina_Tar_Heels_men%27s_basketball "North Carolina Tar Heels men's basketball"). Although the Jayhawks lost by one point in triple overtime, Chamberlain was named the tournament's Most Outstanding Player. Briefly a member of the [Harlem Globetrotters](/wiki/Harlem_Globetrotters "Harlem Globetrotters") before joining the [Philadelphia Warriors](/wiki/Philadelphia_Warriors "Philadelphia Warriors") of the NBA in 1959, Chamberlain won two championships, in 1967 with the [Philadelphia 76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers") and 1972 with the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers"), although his teams were repeatedly defeated by the Celtics in the Eastern Conference and NBA Finals, including two NBA Finals loss to [Willis Reed](/wiki/Willis_Reed "Willis Reed")'s [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks") in 1970 and 1973. He also won seven scoring titles, eleven rebounding titles, and four regular season [Most Valuable Player](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "National Basketball Association Most Valuable Player Award") awards, including the distinction, in 1960, of being the first rookie to receive the award. Stronger than any player of his era, he was usually capable of scoring and rebounding at will. Although he was the target of constant double- and triple-teaming, as well as fouling tactics designed to take advantage of his poor free-throw shooting, he set a number of records that have never been broken. Most notably, Chamberlain is the only player in NBA history to average more than 50 points in a season and [score 100 points in a single game](/wiki/Wilt_Chamberlain%27s_100-point_game "Wilt Chamberlain's 100-point game") (both in 1961–62 as a member of the Philadelphia Warriors). He also holds the NBA's all-time records for rebounding average (27.2), rebounds in a single game (55),[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [career rebounds](/wiki/List_of_National_Basketball_Association_career_rebounding_leaders "List of National Basketball Association career rebounding leaders") (23,924). While Chamberlain excelled in scoring and rebounding, he was also an adept passer for a big man. During his tenure with the 76ers, Chamberlain averaged around 7 assists per game, even leading the league in total assists in the 1967–68 season with 702.

A lesser-known center of the era was [Nate Thurmond](/wiki/Nate_Thurmond "Nate Thurmond"), who initially played the forward position opposite Chamberlain for the San Francisco Warriors but moved to center after Chamberlain was traded to the new Philadelphia franchise. Although he never won a Championship, Thurmond was known as the best [screen](/wiki/Pick_and_roll "Pick and roll") setter in the league, and his averages of 21.3 and 22.0 rebounds per game in 1966–67 and 1967–68, are exceeded only by Chamberlain and Russell.

### 1970s–1980s: Kareem Abdul-Jabbar era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Center_(basketball)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1970s–1980s: Kareem Abdul-Jabbar era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Wes_Unseld_and_Kareem_Abdul-Jabbar.jpeg/250px-Wes_Unseld_and_Kareem_Abdul-Jabbar.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Wes_Unseld_and_Kareem_Abdul-Jabbar.jpeg)

[Kareem Abdul-Jabbar](/wiki/Kareem_Abdul-Jabbar "Kareem Abdul-Jabbar") (right) against [Wes Unseld](/wiki/Wes_Unseld "Wes Unseld") (left)

In contrast to the Celtics dynasty of the 1960s, the 1970s were a decade of parity in the NBA, with eight different champions and no back-to-back winners. At the college level, the [UCLA Bruins](/wiki/UCLA_Bruins_men%27s_basketball "UCLA Bruins men's basketball"), under Coach [John Wooden](/wiki/John_Wooden "John Wooden"), built the greatest dynasty in NCAA basketball history, winning seven consecutive titles between 1967 and 1973. UCLA had already won two consecutive titles in 1964 and 1965 with teams that pressed and emphasized guard play. After not winning in 1966, Wooden's teams changed their style when [Lew Alcindor](/wiki/Lew_Alcindor "Lew Alcindor") became eligible. He led UCLA to three championships-in 1967, '68 and '69-while winning the first [Naismith College Player of the Year](/wiki/Naismith_College_Player_of_the_Year "Naismith College Player of the Year") Award. During his college career, the NCAA enacted a ban on dunking primarily because of Alcindor's dominant use of the shot.[[5]](#cite_note-5) His entrance into the NBA with the [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks") in 1969 was timely, as Bill Russell had just retired and Wilt Chamberlain was 33 years old and increasingly plagued by injuries. After leading the Bucks to the 1971 NBA championship, teamed up with legendary [point guard](/wiki/Point_guard "Point guard") [Oscar Robertson](/wiki/Oscar_Robertson "Oscar Robertson"), Alcindor, who had converted to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), changed his name to [Kareem Abdul-Jabbar](/wiki/Kareem_Abdul-Jabbar "Kareem Abdul-Jabbar"). In 1975, Abdul-Jabbar was traded to the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers"), and, after the arrival of [point guard](/wiki/Point_guard "Point guard") [Magic Johnson](/wiki/Magic_Johnson "Magic Johnson") in 1980, formed part of a new Lakers dynasty that won five NBA titles (1980, '82, '85, '87, '88). In addition, Abdul-Jabbar won six regular-season MVP awards (1971, '72, '74, '76, '77, '80). At 7'2" (2.18 m), 235 pounds (106 kg), he lacked the strength of Chamberlain in his prime, but had a longer wingspan and ultimately proved more durable, adhering to a strict physical-fitness regime that enabled him to play for twenty years, the longest career in NBA history at the time. Offensively, he was best known for his trademark 'Skyhook' hook-shot, almost impossible to block because of his height and wingspan. His regular season career scoring total of 38,387 points was an NBA record for nearly 39 years.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Defensively, the 1973–74 season was the first in which the number of blocked shots were kept, and, over the next seven years, Kareem led the league in this statistic or finished second.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Another product of John Wooden's UCLA program, [Bill Walton](/wiki/Bill_Walton "Bill Walton"), appeared poised to join the ranks of great centers. He led UCLA to back-to-back NCAA titles in 1972 and 1973, he also led the [Portland Trail Blazers](/wiki/Portland_Trail_Blazers "Portland Trail Blazers") to the NBA championship in 1977, and won the NBA MVP the following year. However, his career was plagued with injuries, most infamously a broken bone in his left foot suffered during his MVP season that he never fully recovered from, and he spent most of the following decade on the bench, although he eventually did win a second NBA title as a backup for the [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics") in 1986, when he received the [Sixth Man Award](/wiki/Sixth_Man_Award "Sixth Man Award"). [Willis Reed](/wiki/Willis_Reed "Willis Reed") won two championships with the [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks") in 1970 and 1973, teamed with point guard [Walt Frazier](/wiki/Walt_Frazier "Walt Frazier"); although undersized for the center position at 6'9", he had the strength to play inside, was a highly skilled jump shooter and was effective at setting [picks](/wiki/Pick_and_roll "Pick and roll"), a key element in the Knicks motion-oriented offense. The undersized but scrappy [Dave Cowens](/wiki/Dave_Cowens "Dave Cowens"), drafted at the recommendation of Bill Russell, helped the [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics") win two more NBA titles, in 1974 and 1976.

Leading centers of the late 1970s and early 1980s include [Wes Unseld](/wiki/Wes_Unseld "Wes Unseld") of the [Baltimore/Washington Bullets](/wiki/Washington_Wizards "Washington Wizards"), [Artis Gilmore](/wiki/Artis_Gilmore "Artis Gilmore") of the [ABA](/wiki/American_Basketball_Association "American Basketball Association") [Kentucky Colonels](/wiki/Kentucky_Colonels "Kentucky Colonels"), [Chicago Bulls](/wiki/Chicago_Bulls "Chicago Bulls") and [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs"); [Moses Malone](/wiki/Moses_Malone "Moses Malone") of the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/Houston_Rockets "Houston Rockets") and [Philadelphia 76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers"); and [Robert Parish](/wiki/Robert_Parish "Robert Parish") of the [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics"), who was acquired from the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors") in [1980](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_NBA_season "1980–81 NBA season") for the top overall pick in the NBA Draft. Unseld led the Bullets to four NBA finals appearances and one championship, in 1978. Using his strength and determination to compensate for his lack of size (6'7", i.e. 2.00 m), he was famous for his rebounding, shotblocking, and bone-jarring picks. [Artis Gilmore](/wiki/Artis_Gilmore "Artis Gilmore"), often overlooked because of the mediocrity of his teams, established himself as the best low-post scorer in the league. He set the NCAA Division I record for career average in rebounds (25.2) at tiny [Jacksonville University](/wiki/Jacksonville_University "Jacksonville University"), and enjoyed an illustrious [ABA](/wiki/American_Basketball_Association "American Basketball Association") career before joining the NBA's Chicago Bulls in 1976, playing there until he was traded to San Antonio in 1982, for whom he played until his retirement in 1987. He remains the NBA's career leader in field goal percentage (minimum 2000 shots made) with a 59.9 percentage.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Malone, the first high school player to turn professional, was drafted by the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/Houston_Rockets "Houston Rockets") after several years in the ABA, and won two MVP Awards and led Houston to its first NBA Finals in 1981, before joining the [Philadelphia 76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers"), where, teamed with [Julius Erving](/wiki/Julius_Erving "Julius Erving") and [Bobby Jones](/wiki/Bobby_Jones_(basketball,_born_1951) "Bobby Jones (basketball, born 1951)"), he won an NBA Championship in 1983, as well as a third League MVP. Never a dominant defender, his quickness and tenacity made him one of the best rebounders in NBA history, particularly on the offensive end; he led the league in rebounds six times in a seven-year period and still holds the NBA record for offensive rebounds.

In the mid-1980s, the 7 ft 4 in (2.24 m) [Mark Eaton](/wiki/Mark_Eaton "Mark Eaton") was the most prolific shot-blocker in the league, and, although never a major offensive contributor, won two [NBA Defensive Player of the Year Awards](/wiki/NBA_Defensive_Player_of_the_Year_Award "NBA Defensive Player of the Year Award") while helping transform the lowly [Utah Jazz](/wiki/Utah_Jazz "Utah Jazz") into a playoff contender. Of all these players, none enjoyed the success of [Robert Parish](/wiki/Robert_Parish "Robert Parish"), who, with forwards [Larry Bird](/wiki/Larry_Bird "Larry Bird") and [Kevin McHale](/wiki/Kevin_McHale_(basketball) "Kevin McHale (basketball)"), formed the legendary frontcourt of the [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics") team that won three titles (1981, 1984 and 1986). The Celtics' fierce rivalry with the Lakers dominated the NBA during the decade and helped basketball reach an unprecedented level of popularity. Nicknamed "Chief" after a character in the film *[One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest](/wiki/One_Flew_Over_the_Cuckoo%27s_Nest_(film) "One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest (film)")* because of his stoic demeanor, Parish was known for his trademark arching jump-shot—leading many experts to consider him the best medium-range shooting center of all time[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]—and his ability to finish fast-breaks with his surprising speed. Playing until the age of 43, Parish broke Abdul-Jabbar's record for career games played.

The [1979–80 season](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_NBA_season "1979–80 NBA season") saw the introduction of the [three-point shot](/wiki/Three-point_shot "Three-point shot"). While the concept of the 'stretch five' would not come until the new millennium, players such as [Bill Laimbeer](/wiki/Bill_Laimbeer "Bill Laimbeer") and [Jack Sikma](/wiki/Jack_Sikma "Jack Sikma") developed a reliable outside shot later in their career, hitting around the 30% mark.

### 1990s: Hakeem Olajuwon–Patrick Ewing–David Robinson–Shaquille O'Neal era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Center_(basketball)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990s: Hakeem Olajuwon–Patrick Ewing–David Robinson–Shaquille O'Neal era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Hakeem_Olajuwon_20_June_1984_San_Angelo_Standard-Times_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Hakeem_Olajuwon_20_June_1984_San_Angelo_Standard-Times_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hakeem_Olajuwon_20_June_1984_San_Angelo_Standard-Times_(cropped).jpg)

[Hakeem Olajuwon](/wiki/Hakeem_Olajuwon "Hakeem Olajuwon")

Coach [John Thompson](/wiki/John_Thompson_(basketball) "John Thompson (basketball)"), once a seldom-used backup to [Bill Russell](/wiki/Bill_Russell "Bill Russell") with the Celtics, developed the [Georgetown Hoyas](/wiki/Georgetown_Hoyas_men%27s_basketball "Georgetown Hoyas men's basketball") into a chief pipeline for talent at the Center position, producing a succession of great defensive big men in [Patrick Ewing](/wiki/Patrick_Ewing "Patrick Ewing"), [Dikembe Mutombo](/wiki/Dikembe_Mutombo "Dikembe Mutombo") and [Alonzo Mourning](/wiki/Alonzo_Mourning "Alonzo Mourning"). In 1984, Georgetown, led by Patrick Ewing, defeated the [University of Houston](/wiki/University_of_Houston "University of Houston"), led by [Hakeem Olajuwon](/wiki/Hakeem_Olajuwon "Hakeem Olajuwon"), to win the NCAA championship. The number one picks in the [1984](/wiki/1984_NBA_draft "1984 NBA draft"), [1985](/wiki/1985_NBA_draft "1985 NBA draft"), and [1987](/wiki/1987_NBA_draft "1987 NBA draft") NBA Drafts would all be used on centers who made major impacts in the NBA of the 1990s and eventually became members of the Basketball Hall of Fame: Olajuwon in 1984, Ewing in 1985, and [Navy's](/wiki/Navy_Midshipmen_men%27s_basketball "Navy Midshipmen men's basketball") [David Robinson](/wiki/David_Robinson "David Robinson") in 1987.

The [Nigerian](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria")-born Olajuwon was drafted by the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/Houston_Rockets "Houston Rockets") and paired with [power forward](/wiki/Power_forward "Power forward") [Ralph Sampson](/wiki/Ralph_Sampson "Ralph Sampson") in what was dubbed the 'Twin Towers' duo. In his second season, 1985–86, the Rockets upset the Lakers in the Western Conference finals. Olajuwon established himself as a dominant player, leading the Rockets to two consecutive NBA championships in 1994 and 1995. In the 1993–94 season he became the only player in NBA history to win the NBA's [Most Valuable Player](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "National Basketball Association Most Valuable Player Award") (MVP), [Defensive Player of the Year](/wiki/NBA_Defensive_Player_of_the_Year "NBA Defensive Player of the Year"), and [Finals MVP](/wiki/Finals_MVP "Finals MVP") awards in the same season. Defensively Olajuwon broke Abdul-Jabbar's career record for blocked shots. Offensively, he was best known for his 'Dream Shake', a series of fakes and spin moves regarded as the pinnacle of big man footwork.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Ewing, from [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") by way of the [Boston area](/wiki/Boston_area "Boston area"), was drafted by the [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks") in 1985, with whom he spent fifteen of his seventeen seasons in the NBA. An eleven-time All Star, Ewing was one of the best shooting centers in NBA history, possessing a baseline jump-shot, as well as being a formidable shot blocker and rebounder. The Knicks were formidable opponents of the [Chicago Bulls](/wiki/Chicago_Bulls "Chicago Bulls") dynasty in the Eastern Conference playoffs. Ewing's successor as the starting center at Georgetown, [Dikembe Mutombo](/wiki/Dikembe_Mutombo "Dikembe Mutombo"), who played most of his NBA career with the [Denver Nuggets](/wiki/Denver_Nuggets "Denver Nuggets") and [Atlanta Hawks](/wiki/Atlanta_Hawks "Atlanta Hawks") proved a dominant defender. Mutombo, who had not played basketball before arriving in the U.S. from his native [Zaire](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") on a [USAID](/wiki/USAID "USAID") scholarship, was among the greatest shot blockers in NBA history, leading the NBA in blocked-shots five consecutive years, in the course of a career in which he ranked second in the history of the league in blocked shots, behind only Hakeem Olajuwon. He was also the recipient of four [NBA Defensive Player of the Year Awards](/wiki/NBA_Defensive_Player_of_the_Year_Award "NBA Defensive Player of the Year Award"), tied for the record with [Ben Wallace](/wiki/Ben_Wallace_(basketball) "Ben Wallace (basketball)").

Robinson, drafted by the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs") in 1987, did not enter the NBA for two years due to his commitment with the [United States Naval Academy](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Academy "United States Naval Academy"). Upon his arrival in 1989, Robinson instantly transformed the Spurs into title contenders, eventually becoming a ten-time All-Star, MVP and Defensive Player of the Year. Leaner but more muscular than most centers, Robinson outran opponents with his speed and agility, while possessing a reliable left-handed jumper.

The [1992 NBA draft](/wiki/1992_NBA_draft "1992 NBA draft") marked the entrance into the league of [Shaquille O'Neal](/wiki/Shaquille_O%27Neal "Shaquille O'Neal"), who was drafted by the [Orlando Magic](/wiki/Orlando_Magic "Orlando Magic"). Immediately drawing comparisons to Wilt Chamberlain, the 7 ft 1 in (2.16 m), 325-pound (147 kg) O'Neal was billed as potentially the most physically dominating player ever. By his third season, he led the league in scoring and led the Magic to the NBA Finals, where they were swept by the [Houston Rockets](/wiki/Houston_Rockets "Houston Rockets"). After the 1995–96 season, he signed with the rebuilding [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers"). Former Georgetown center [Alonzo Mourning](/wiki/Alonzo_Mourning "Alonzo Mourning"), also drafted in 1992, established himself as a premier big man with the [Charlotte Hornets](/wiki/Charlotte_Hornets "Charlotte Hornets") and, later, the [Miami Heat](/wiki/Miami_Heat "Miami Heat"), winning two Defensive Player of the Year Awards thanks to his prolific shot blocking while also proving a reliable scoring threat.

### 2000s: Changing times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Center_(basketball)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000s: Changing times")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/65/Lipofsky_Shaquille_O%27Neal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lipofsky_Shaquille_O%27Neal.jpg)

[Shaquille O'Neal](/wiki/Shaquille_O%27Neal "Shaquille O'Neal")

The NBA landscape began to change in the 1990s as more international players entered the league. [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia")'s [Vlade Divac](/wiki/Vlade_Divac "Vlade Divac") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania")'s [Arvydas Sabonis](/wiki/Arvydas_Sabonis "Arvydas Sabonis") arrived in 1989 and 1995 respectively, and brought in a new play style. Unlike traditional post-up centers, Divac and Sabonis focused more on playmaking and perimeter shooting, hallmarks of the European style of play. In contrast to American players who were trained based on their size and individual athletic ability, European players were honed for their fundamentals regardless of size, and play a more team-oriented style of basketball.

The balance of power shifted to the Western Conference, and the NBA was dominated by the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers"), who won titles in 2000, 2001, 2002, 2009, and 2010, and the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs"), who won in 1999, 2003, 2005, 2007, and 2014. Even as traditional centers such as Shaquille O'Neal and later on [Yao Ming](/wiki/Yao_Ming "Yao Ming") and [Dwight Howard](/wiki/Dwight_Howard "Dwight Howard") continued to dominate the game, the importance of a center was starting to change.

In the 1990s, an increasing number of smaller forwards, most notably [Dennis Rodman](/wiki/Dennis_Rodman "Dennis Rodman") and [Charles Barkley](/wiki/Charles_Barkley "Charles Barkley"), excelled at the traditional center functions of rebounding, shot-blocking and low-post defense, anticipating a trend towards relying on shorter and quicker post players that continued into the 2000s, as exemplified by perennial Defensive Player of the Year [Ben Wallace](/wiki/Ben_Wallace_(basketball) "Ben Wallace (basketball)"). In the [fast break](/wiki/Fast_break "Fast break") oriented style of offense employed by a growing number of teams, the traditional role of the center is diminished, if not done away with altogether. Many talented big men choose to play the more versatile [power forward](/wiki/Power_forward "Power forward") position, giving them more room to run the floor and play outside the paint. Under the influence of European basketball, the offensive role of big men has been redefined to include more emphasis on perimeter play, as exemplified by 3-point shooting big men like [Dirk Nowitzki](/wiki/Dirk_Nowitzki "Dirk Nowitzki"), [Mehmet Okur](/wiki/Mehmet_Okur "Mehmet Okur"), [Andrea Bargnani](/wiki/Andrea_Bargnani "Andrea Bargnani"), and [Channing Frye](/wiki/Channing_Frye "Channing Frye").

Most importantly, winning an NBA championship no longer required a dominant center, unlike in previous years. Perimeter players such as [Larry Bird](/wiki/Larry_Bird "Larry Bird"), [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan"), [Kobe Bryant](/wiki/Kobe_Bryant "Kobe Bryant"), [LeBron James](/wiki/LeBron_James "LeBron James"), [Kevin Durant](/wiki/Kevin_Durant "Kevin Durant"), and [Stephen Curry](/wiki/Stephen_Curry "Stephen Curry") became the faces of modern championship teams, and in those instances, their centers were more of a complementary piece than a cornerstone.

### 2010s–present: Rise of stretch five and playmaking centers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Center_(basketball)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010s–present: Rise of stretch five and playmaking centers")]

The low-post functions of the center position continued to dwindle in the 2010s as the NBA embraced [small ball](/wiki/Small_ball_(basketball) "Small ball (basketball)") and a more perimeter-oriented style of play. While there are still a few centers that play a more traditional style, some centers begin to adapt to the outside game.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Nikola_Jokic_free_throw_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Nikola_Jokic_free_throw_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nikola_Jokic_free_throw_(cropped).jpg)

[Nikola Jokić](/wiki/Nikola_Joki%C4%87 "Nikola Jokić")

The [Miami Heat](/wiki/Miami_Heat "Miami Heat"), for example, featured a "positionless" lineup as coined by head coach [Erik Spoelstra](/wiki/Erik_Spoelstra "Erik Spoelstra"), with power forwards [Chris Bosh](/wiki/Chris_Bosh "Chris Bosh") and [Udonis Haslem](/wiki/Udonis_Haslem "Udonis Haslem") shifting between center and power forward depending on matchups. The Heat won two NBA championships in [2012](/wiki/2012_NBA_Finals "2012 NBA Finals") and [2013](/wiki/2013_NBA_Finals "2013 NBA Finals") using this unconventional lineup.

The [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors") then won three championships in [2015](/wiki/2015_NBA_Finals "2015 NBA Finals"), [2017](/wiki/2017_NBA_Finals "2017 NBA Finals"), and [2018](/wiki/2018_NBA_Finals "2018 NBA Finals") with a smaller and quicker group known as the [Death Lineup](/wiki/Death_Lineup "Death Lineup"). In this case, 6'7" forward [Draymond Green](/wiki/Draymond_Green "Draymond Green") was often slotted into the center position, creating matchup problems for larger centers with his defensive versatility, speed, ball-handling skills, and perimeter shooting.

The rise of the stretch five paved the way for centers to add outside shooting to their arsenal. While they continue to roam the paint at times, they have also expanded their game to the perimeter with their three-point shots. Notable modern stretch fives include [Al Horford](/wiki/Al_Horford "Al Horford"), [Joel Embiid](/wiki/Joel_Embiid "Joel Embiid"), [Karl-Anthony Towns](/wiki/Karl-Anthony_Towns "Karl-Anthony Towns"), [Brook Lopez](/wiki/Brook_Lopez "Brook Lopez"), [Marc Gasol](/wiki/Marc_Gasol "Marc Gasol"), and [Kristaps Porziņģis](/wiki/Kristaps_Porzi%C5%86%C4%A3is "Kristaps Porziņģis").

Another byproduct of the small ball revolution is the emergence of big men who possess guard-like skills. An example is [Nikola Jokić](/wiki/Nikola_Joki%C4%87 "Nikola Jokić"), whose passing ability for a player his size made the Denver Nuggets contenders in the late 2010s and early 2020s, winning their first ever NBA Championship in [2023](/wiki/2023_NBA_Finals "2023 NBA Finals"). [Victor Wembanyama](/wiki/Victor_Wembanyama "Victor Wembanyama"), at 7'4", also emerged as a highly touted prospect by dominating the [French professional leagues](/wiki/LNB_%C3%89lite "LNB Élite"), before getting drafted by the San Antonio Spurs as the first pick of the [2023 NBA draft](/wiki/2023_NBA_draft "2023 NBA draft").

Before 2021, the most recent center to win the NBA MVP award was Shaquille O'Neal in 2000. During the first half of the 2020s, centers won a combined four MVP awards, with Jokić winning three awards in 2021, 2022 and 2024, and Embiid in 2023.

## In women's basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Center_(basketball)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: In women's basketball")]

In women's basketball, 7 ft 0 in (2.13 m) [Uljana Semjonova](/wiki/Uljana_Semjonova "Uljana Semjonova") played the center position, helping the Soviet Union women's team win two Olympic gold medals, in 1976 and 1980, and never losing a game in international competition. Semjonova also led her club team, TTT Riga to fifteen European Champion's Cup titles. The 6 ft 8 in (2.03 m) [Anne Donovan](/wiki/Anne_Donovan "Anne Donovan") led the [Old Dominion University](/wiki/Old_Dominion_University "Old Dominion University") Lady Monarchs to the 1979 [Association for Intercollegiate Athletics for Women](/wiki/Association_for_Intercollegiate_Athletics_for_Women "Association for Intercollegiate Athletics for Women") championship, and was the first female [Naismith College Player of the Year](/wiki/Naismith_College_Player_of_the_Year "Naismith College Player of the Year") recipient in 1983, before enjoying a successful professional career in Japan and Italy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Margo_Dydek_1.jpg/250px-Margo_Dydek_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Margo_Dydek_1.jpg)

[Margo Dydek](/wiki/Margo_Dydek "Margo Dydek")

With the formation of the [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA"), [Lisa Leslie](/wiki/Lisa_Leslie "Lisa Leslie") established herself as the premier center, and the league's most popular player. The first WNBA player to reach the 3,000 point milestone, she led the [Los Angeles Sparks](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Sparks "Los Angeles Sparks") to consecutive titles in 2001 and 2002, and holds the distinction of being the first player to dunk in a WNBA game. Other prominent centers in women's basketball include Australian [Lauren Jackson](/wiki/Lauren_Jackson "Lauren Jackson") of the [Seattle Storm](/wiki/Seattle_Storm "Seattle Storm") and [Karl Malone](/wiki/Karl_Malone "Karl Malone")'s daughter [Cheryl Ford](/wiki/Cheryl_Ford "Cheryl Ford") of the [Detroit Shock](/wiki/Detroit_Shock "Detroit Shock"). Jackson can also be considered a [forward-center](/wiki/Forward-center "Forward-center") because she is also a very good outside shooter, leading the WNBA in three-point shooting percentage in one season. [Rebecca Lobo](/wiki/Rebecca_Lobo "Rebecca Lobo") led the [Connecticut Huskies](/wiki/Connecticut_Huskies "Connecticut Huskies") to an [NCAA Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Women%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Women's Division I Basketball Championship") in 1995, but never recovered from a [torn ACL](/wiki/Torn_ACL "Torn ACL") and had a disappointing professional career. When at the [University of Oklahoma](/wiki/University_of_Oklahoma "University of Oklahoma"), [Courtney Paris](/wiki/Courtney_Paris "Courtney Paris") was considered the next dominant female center after becoming the only NCAA player, male or female, to score 700 points, grab 500 rebounds and block 100 shots in a single season; however, she had a disappointing WNBA career and was waived before what would have been the start of her second season in 2010. The tallest center in the WNBA, the late [Margo Dydek](/wiki/Margo_Dydek "Margo Dydek") of Poland, who stood 7'2", is still the league's career leader in blocks per game (2.72) and total blocks (877), solidly ahead of Leslie in that category despite Dydek playing 40 fewer games before her retirement.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
